name: Atrial Flutter
identifyBy: Sawtooth flutter waves in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) - no isoelectric baseline
Regular ventricular response - most commonly 2:1 (rate ~150 bpm)
Flutter waves best seen at slower ventricular rates (4:1) or with adenosine (slows AV conduction transiently)
Regular rate of exactly 150 bpm: always suspect 2:1 flutter

measurements.rate: Ventricular: 75–150 bpm (depends on ratio); Atrial: 250–350 bpm
measurements.pWaves: Sawtooth flutter waves - negative in II, III, aVF
measurements.prInterval: Fixed if regular AV conduction ratio
measurements.stSegment: Cannot assess - hidden by flutter waves

management: Rate control: beta-blocker or diltiazem (IV) for rapid rate
Anticoagulation if >48 hours duration or uncertain - AF/flutter stroke risk similar
DC cardioversion 50–100J for cardioversion (flutter is very cardioversion-sensitive)
Flecainide or Amiodarone for chemical cardioversion
Definitive: catheter ablation of cavotricuspid isthmus (>95% cure rate for typical flutter)
pearls: Rate of exactly 150 bpm = think 2:1 flutter first - P waves hidden in T waves
Adenosine can unmask flutter waves by transiently blocking AV conduction
Typical (type I) flutter: negative flutter waves in II, III, aVF; positive in V1
Atrial flutter is NOT the same as AF - flutter is organised, a re-entry macro-circuit
Flutter can conduct 1:1 at >300 bpm if AV node is sympathetically stimulated - dangerous
differentials: Atrial tachycardia (discrete P waves, isoelectric baseline)
